text: Hello, welcome to another edition of This is My Architecture. My name is Ryan and I'm joined by
Jonathan from Rappi. Jonathan, welcome to This is My Architecture. Thanks for having me. So tell
us a little bit about what Rappi does. Rappi is a Latin America super app offering users fast
delivery of a range of products from restaurant food, supermarket, and pharmacy, a suite of financial
services and a platform for booking experiences like concerts, hotel stays, and flights. Great.
We have an architectural diagram in front of us right here. Before we dive into this,
let's talk about Rappi Pay, which is what this pertains to. Rappi Pay is the financial vertical
of Rappi. It started three years ago and it grew, became exponential pretty quick. We had a pretty
small team then. We knew that we have to build a platform simple to use for a quickly growing
developer team. Scalable, reliable, secure, and cost efficient. So we built this platform
that is mostly based on Kubernetes. We started using K-OPS as our Kubernetes engine. But as
we scaled, we noticed that managing the Kubernetes control plane was a hard operational world for
us. So we quickly moved to EKS. Our EKS clusters are composed by Spot instances and for on-demand
instances. That was key for us for scaling cost efficiently. We were using Spot In for that operation.
And that was great for not having to manage that part of the scaling to make this cost efficient.
Okay. Makes sense. And you've got some other pieces here, components. So can you walk us
through what each of these might mean and how they interact with the architecture?
Yes. Our end users comes into our microservices through CloudFront. We are using CloudFront for
early TLS termination and also for caching. We also have WAF here. And then the traffic goes into our
ingress controllers through to ALB, into EKS. And the traffic between our microservices inside the cluster
and across clusters is happening in the mesh. And we also have, we need to communicate with several partners. And that's mostly taking place using Direct Connect here.
Okay, great. You've also got a suite of services down here that looks like a persistent layer. Talk to us a little bit more about this layer here of the architecture.
Yes. I mentioned that the platform should be simple to use for developers. So we created our custom microservices API where developers can handle the entire lifecycle of their microservices.

They can create microservices. They can deploy them. And they can also create databases, relational databases, document databases. If they need a cache, they can create a cache. And if they need to publish to a topic or consume, they can make all the things happen through this layer.
And it's great for us having AWS managed services because we can focus on adding value in this layer instead of taking care of the control of the underlying and scaling and making that reliable.

That makes sense. Yeah. You want to hide away a lot of that complexity from your developers to make it simple and easy to scale and for them to use as well. So of course, Kubernetes and ECU are powerful technologies.
There's a lot of things that could be run at scale. So developers doesn't even know where the applications are running or in which RDS instance their data is living.
Makes sense. And in terms of your transactions and your throughput, what sorts of numbers are you seeing now with this sort of architecture?
Today, this architecture is processing around thousands of transactions per hour. We have around 15,000 bots running to 300 developers and 400 deployments per day with this platform.
Fantastic. That's awesome growth. Very cool to see that you've built this architecture and the tremendous growth that you're seeing within RappiPay.
You should be very well served for years to come. So thank you, Jonathan. And thank you all for watching. This is my architecture.
